title: CAC Payback: Bootstrapped vs VC Benchmarks

questions.question: What is a healthy CAC payback period for a bootstrapped business?
questions.explanation: Under 3 months is healthy for bootstrapped businesses. 3-6 months is the caution zone. 6-12 months is dangerous bootstrapped. Over 12 months is essentially only viable with substantial funding.

questions.question: What is the correct CAC Payback Period formula?
questions.explanation: CAC Payback Period = CAC / (ARPU x Gross Margin %). You divide your acquisition cost by the monthly profit per customer (revenue adjusted for cost of goods sold), not just revenue.

questions.question: What is the median CAC payback period for VC-backed SaaS companies?
questions.explanation: Median funded SaaS CAC payback is 15-18 months. VC-backed companies can tolerate this because they have investor money to cover the gap. Bootstrapped founders cannot — their cash is finite.

questions.question: When calculating CAC, it's acceptable to exclude your own time cost since you're not paying yourself a salary.
questions.explanation: False. Your time has a market rate even if you're not paying yourself. Marcus thought his CAC was $150 but after including 35 hours/month at $100/hour, his real CAC was $730. Ignoring time cost leads to false confidence.

questions.question: Calculate your full CAC (including time cost) and your CAC payback period. How does your payback compare to the bootstrapped benchmark? What specific action would shorten your payback period?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response calculates CAC with time cost included, computes the payback period using ARPU and gross margin, compares it to the under-3-month bootstrapped benchmark, and proposes a specific action (reduce CAC, increase ARPU, or improve margin) to shorten payback.
